review.note: AI-drafted first pass - SME review required before production

sources.title: Grade 11 - Term 1: Communism in Russia 1900 to 1940

slides.title: Communism in Russia, 1900-1940
slides.lead: How a vast peasant empire ruled by a Tsar became the world's first communist state - and at what human cost.

media.alt: A red star rising over a stylised industrial skyline of factory chimneys.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='320' height='52' fill='#7a5a3a'/><g fill='#4a4a4a'><rect x='40' y='84' width='22' height='44'/><rect x='78' y='66' width='22' height='62'/><rect x='116' y='96' width='22' height='32'/></g><path d='M232 34 l10 30 h32 l-26 19 10 31 -26 -19 -26 19 10 -31 -26 -19 h32 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Frame the unit's central question: how and why did Russia move from tsarist autocracy to a one-party communist state in barely two decades? Preview the three phases learners will analyse - the collapse of Tsarism, the Bolshevik seizure and consolidation of power, and Stalin's transformation of the USSR. Remind learners to weigh both the promises of the revolution and its costs.

slides.title: Tsarist Russia around 1900
slides.lead: An autocratic empire where a tiny elite held power over a poor, mostly peasant population.

blocks.text: Russia was ruled by an **autocrat**, Tsar **Nicholas II** of the Romanov dynasty, who claimed absolute, God-given power and answered to no parliament. Society was steeply unequal: a small nobility and growing middle class sat above millions of impoverished **peasants** and a new urban **working class**.

items: Roughly 80% of the population were peasants, many still deeply poor despite the 1861 emancipation of the serfs.
Rapid industrialisation created crowded cities and harsh factory conditions.
The secret police (Okhrana) and censorship silenced political opposition.

media.alt: A pyramid divided into layers, a narrow top over a wide base, showing an unequal society.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M110 24 L96 60 H124 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M96 60 L82 96 H138 Z' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M82 96 L64 140 H156 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><line x1='0' y1='60' x2='220' y2='60' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='0' y1='96' x2='220' y2='96' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

slides.notes: Stress the structural tensions that made Russia unstable: autocratic rule with no legal outlet for dissent, an enormous impoverished peasantry hungry for land, and a small but militant industrial working class. These are the pressures that revolutionaries would later exploit. Introduce the term autocracy and contrast it with constitutional government.

slides.title: Revolutionary ideas
slides.lead: Opposition to the Tsar drew on competing ideologies, especially Marxism.

blocks.heading: Marxism
blocks.text: Karl Marx argued history is driven by class struggle and that workers (the proletariat) would overthrow the capitalist class.

blocks.heading: Bolsheviks
blocks.text: A radical wing led by Lenin who wanted a tightly organised party to seize power for the workers.

blocks.heading: Mensheviks
blocks.text: A more moderate Marxist wing who believed Russia must pass through capitalism first, with a broader party.

slides.notes: Explain that the Russian Social Democratic Labour Party split in 1903 into Bolsheviks (led by Lenin) and Mensheviks. Keep the distinction simple but accurate: both were Marxist, but they disagreed on party structure and how quickly revolution could come. This split matters because it is the Bolsheviks who eventually take power in 1917.

slides.title: The 1905 Revolution
slides.lead: Defeat in war and a massacre of peaceful protesters shook the Tsar's authority.

blocks.text: Humiliating defeat in the **Russo-Japanese War (1904-1905)** deepened discontent. On **Bloody Sunday** (22 January 1905) troops fired on peaceful marchers petitioning the Tsar in St Petersburg, killing many. Strikes, mutinies and the first workers' councils, or **soviets**, spread across the empire.

items: To survive, Nicholas II issued the October Manifesto promising a parliament (the Duma) and civil rights.
Once order returned, he clawed back these concessions and kept real power.
1905 is often called a 'dress rehearsal' for the revolutions of 1917.

media.alt: A crowd of small figures before palace gates, with a red banner raised.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='150' y='40' width='90' height='96' fill='#c8b89a'/><g fill='#8a8a8a'><rect x='158' y='52' width='8' height='84'/><rect x='176' y='52' width='8' height='84'/><rect x='194' y='52' width='8' height='84'/><rect x='212' y='52' width='8' height='84'/></g><g fill='#4a4a4a'><circle cx='30' cy='104' r='7'/><circle cx='52' cy='100' r='7'/><circle cx='74' cy='106' r='7'/><circle cx='96' cy='100' r='7'/><circle cx='118' cy='106' r='7'/></g><rect x='70' y='60' width='4' height='44' fill='#5a3a24'/><rect x='74' y='60' width='30' height='18' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Use 1905 to show a pattern: crisis (war) plus a trigger (Bloody Sunday) plus economic hardship produces mass unrest. Emphasise that the Tsar survived only by making promises he did not keep, which discredited him further. The soviets appear here for the first time and will reappear in 1917, so highlight them.

slides.title: World War I and the strain on Russia
slides.lead: Total war exposed the weaknesses of the tsarist state and pushed it to breaking point.

blocks.text: Russia entered **World War I** in 1914. Poorly equipped armies suffered huge casualties, while food and fuel shortages gripped the cities. Nicholas II took personal command at the front, leaving government in the hands of the unpopular **Tsarina Alexandra** and the disreputable adviser **Rasputin**.

items: Millions of soldiers were killed, wounded or captured.
Inflation and shortages caused hunger and long bread queues in Petrograd.
By early 1917 loyalty to the Tsar had collapsed among workers, soldiers and even elites.

media.alt: A rising line graph showing hardship increasing towards 1917.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><line x1='30' y1='20' x2='30' y2='134' stroke='#4a4a4a' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='134' x2='214' y2='134' stroke='#4a4a4a' stroke-width='2'/><polyline points='34,120 78,108 122,84 166,52 208,26' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4'/><text x='120' y='152' font-size='11' fill='#4a4a4a' text-anchor='middle'>1914 to 1917</text></svg>

slides.notes: Frame WWI as the catalyst that turned chronic problems into total collapse. The key idea for FET learners is causation: the war did not create Russia's problems but massively intensified them and stripped the Tsar of his remaining support. Note the loss of the army's loyalty - without the soldiers, the regime could not defend itself.

slides.title: The fall of the Tsar
slides.lead: In March 1917 (February by the old Russian calendar) strikes and mutinies swept away the monarchy.

blocks.text: Bread protests and strikes in **Petrograd** grew into mass demonstrations. When soldiers refused to fire on the crowds and joined them, the regime lost control. On **15 March 1917 (2 March, old calendar)** Nicholas II **abdicated**, ending over 300 years of Romanov rule.

items: A **Provisional Government** of liberal and moderate politicians took charge.
At the same time the **Petrograd Soviet** of workers and soldiers held real influence.
This overlap became known as **Dual Power** - two authorities competing to govern.

media.alt: A crown tipping and falling off a pedestal.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='78' y='110' width='44' height='30' fill='#c8b89a'/><g transform='rotate(24 130 70)'><path d='M104 78 L112 50 L124 66 L136 44 L148 66 L160 50 L168 78 Z' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect x='104' y='78' width='64' height='10' fill='#c98a1f'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Clarify the calendar confusion up front: Russia used the Julian calendar, so the 'February' Revolution happened in March by our calendar, and the 'October' Revolution in November. Emphasise that February was a largely spontaneous, popular rising, not a Bolshevik plot. Introduce Dual Power carefully - it explains why the Provisional Government was weak and short-lived.

slides.title: Lenin and the Bolsheviks
slides.lead: Lenin returned from exile with a sharp, simple programme that spoke to a war-weary, hungry population.

blocks.text: Returning in April 1917, **Vladimir Lenin** rejected any cooperation with the Provisional Government. In his **April Theses** he demanded an immediate end to the war, land for the peasants, and 'All power to the Soviets'. As the Provisional Government kept fighting the war, Bolshevik support grew.

items: The Provisional Government's decision to continue WWI was deeply unpopular.
Lenin promised what people most wanted: peace, land and food.
The Bolsheviks won majorities in the Petrograd and Moscow soviets by autumn 1917.

slides.notes: Explain why the Bolsheviks succeeded where the Provisional Government failed: they offered clear, popular solutions to the war, land hunger and food shortages, while the Provisional Government kept fighting an unwinnable war. The slogan 'Peace, Land, Bread' is a superb example of political messaging - ask learners why each word mattered to a specific group.

slides.title: The Bolsheviks seize power
slides.lead: In November 1917 (October by the old calendar) the Bolsheviks overthrew the Provisional Government.

blocks.text: Organised largely by **Leon Trotsky** through the Petrograd Soviet's Military Revolutionary Committee, armed workers, soldiers and sailors (the Red Guards) seized key points in Petrograd and stormed the **Winter Palace** on **7 November 1917 (25 October, old calendar)**. The Provisional Government fell with little resistance.

items: The Bolsheviks issued decrees on peace and on land almost immediately.
This was a disciplined seizure of power, not a mass popular uprising like February.
Russia became the world's first state ruled in the name of communism.

media.alt: A red flag raised over a large palace building at night.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#2c2c3a'/><circle cx='198' cy='34' r='14' fill='#e8e2d0'/><rect x='30' y='72' width='180' height='64' fill='#6b6b5a'/><g fill='#2c2c3a'><rect x='44' y='86' width='12' height='28'/><rect x='72' y='86' width='12' height='28'/><rect x='100' y='86' width='12' height='28'/><rect x='128' y='86' width='12' height='28'/><rect x='156' y='86' width='12' height='28'/><rect x='184' y='86' width='12' height='28'/></g><rect x='118' y='30' width='4' height='44' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='122' y='30' width='34' height='20' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Draw the crucial contrast: February was a broad, spontaneous collapse of the monarchy; October was a planned, tightly organised takeover by a disciplined minority party. Credit Trotsky's organising role. Note that seizing power in Petrograd was one thing - holding power across a vast, divided country would trigger a brutal civil war, the next slide.

slides.title: Civil War and War Communism
slides.lead: The Bolsheviks won a devastating civil war but at enormous human cost.

blocks.text: Opponents of the Bolsheviks - the **Whites**, a loose alliance of monarchists, liberals and rival socialists, backed by foreign powers - fought the Bolshevik **Reds**. Trotsky built and led the **Red Army**. To supply it, the government imposed **War Communism**, seizing grain from peasants and controlling the economy, while the secret police (**Cheka**) crushed opposition through the 'Red Terror'.

items: The former Tsar and his family were executed in 1918.
War, famine and disease killed millions between 1918 and 1921.
The Reds won by 1921, helped by unity, control of the centre and railways, and White disunity.

media.alt: Two opposing arrows, red and grey, meeting in the middle of a map outline.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='30' y='40' width='180' height='80' rx='10' fill='#e6ddc9'/><path d='M40 80 h120 l-16 -14 v9 h-104 z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M40 80 h104 v9 l16 -14 h-120 z' fill='#8a1f1f' opacity='0'/><path d='M200 80 h-70 l16 -14 v9 h54 z' fill='#6b6b6b'/><path d='M200 80 h-54 v9 l-16 -14 h70 z' fill='#6b6b6b' opacity='0'/></svg>

slides.notes: Handle this violent period responsibly: be factual about the scale of suffering without dwelling on gratuitous detail. Explain why the Reds won - central position, control of railways and industry, a single command under Trotsky, and the disunity of the Whites. Introduce War Communism and the Cheka, because peasant anger at grain seizures leads directly to the NEP on the next slide.

slides.title: The NEP and the birth of the USSR
slides.lead: Lenin retreated to a mixed economy to recover, and a new union was formed.

blocks.text: After famine and revolts (including the 1921 **Kronstadt** mutiny), Lenin introduced the **New Economic Policy (NEP)** in 1921. It allowed small private trade and let peasants sell surplus grain, while the state kept control of major industry. In **1922** the **Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR)** was formally created.

items: The NEP was a pragmatic compromise to rebuild a shattered economy.
Some communists disliked it as a retreat from socialism.
Lenin died in January 1924, leaving no clear successor.

media.alt: A crossed hammer and sickle emblem inside a circle.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 180 160' role='img'><rect width='180' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='90' cy='80' r='52' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M64 108 q22 -50 44 -66' stroke='#e8d9a0' stroke-width='9' fill='none' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M108 42 q18 12 8 34' stroke='#e8d9a0' stroke-width='9' fill='none' stroke-linecap='round'/><rect x='60' y='96' width='52' height='9' rx='3' fill='#e8d9a0' transform='rotate(-38 86 100)'/><rect x='96' y='58' width='28' height='12' rx='2' fill='#e8d9a0' transform='rotate(-38 110 64)'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the NEP as a strategic retreat: Lenin accepted limited capitalism to save the revolution after the disasters of War Communism. Note the debate this opened inside the party about how far to compromise - a debate Stalin and Trotsky would later fight over. Lenin's death without a named successor sets up the power struggle on the next slide.

slides.title: Stalin's rise to power
slides.lead: The quiet party administrator outmanoeuvred his rivals to become sole leader.

blocks.text: **Joseph Stalin**, as General Secretary of the party, controlled appointments and built a network of loyal officials. Playing rivals against one another, he defeated the more famous **Leon Trotsky**, who was expelled from the party and later exiled and assassinated. By around 1928 Stalin was the undisputed leader of the USSR.

items: Stalin argued for 'Socialism in One Country'; Trotsky favoured worldwide revolution.
Stalin's control of the party machine was his decisive advantage.
He then launched a drastic programme to transform the Soviet economy.

media.alt: Two chess pieces, one standing and one toppled, on a board.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='30' y='118' width='160' height='16' fill='#c8b89a'/><g fill='#4a4a4a'><rect x='70' y='60' width='16' height='58'/><circle cx='78' cy='56' r='12'/></g><g fill='#8a1f1f'><rect x='120' y='110' width='48' height='14' rx='6'/><circle cx='166' cy='116' r='10'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Correct a common misconception: Stalin won not because he was the most brilliant theorist - Trotsky was more celebrated - but because he controlled the party's administrative machinery and appointments. Explain 'Socialism in One Country' versus 'permanent revolution' simply. Set up the turn from politics to economics: Stalin now reshapes Soviet society by force.

slides.title: Collectivisation and industrialisation
slides.lead: Stalin drove rapid modernisation through the Five-Year Plans and forced collective farming.

columns.text: Peasants were forced to give up private land and join large **collective farms (kolkhozy)**. Wealthier peasants, labelled **kulaks**, were dispossessed, deported or killed.

items: Grain was seized to feed cities and to export.
Resistance and disruption helped cause a catastrophic famine in 1932-1933.

columns.text: The **Five-Year Plans** (from 1928) set huge targets for coal, steel, oil and machinery, building new industrial cities almost from nothing.

items: Output of heavy industry rose dramatically.
Workers faced harsh discipline, poor conditions and forced labour.

slides.notes: Use the two-column layout to let learners compare the two arms of Stalin's economic revolution. Be honest about the trade-off central to CAPS analysis: the USSR industrialised at extraordinary speed, which later helped it survive WWII, but this was achieved through coercion, mass suffering and famine. The famine in Ukraine and other regions in 1932-1933 (the Holodomor) is covered next; treat it with care.

slides.title: Famine, terror and the purges
slides.lead: Stalin's rule combined rapid modernisation with mass repression and death.

blocks.text: The famine of **1932-1933**, worst in Ukraine (often called the **Holodomor**), killed millions. During the **Great Purge** (1936-1938), Stalin turned on his own party, army and society: 'show trials' condemned old Bolsheviks, and vast numbers were executed or sent to forced-labour camps, the **Gulag**.

items: Fear and denunciation were used to control the population.
A cult of personality presented Stalin as an all-wise leader.
By 1940 the USSR was a highly industrialised but deeply repressive dictatorship.

media.alt: A field of empty stalks under a grey sky, suggesting famine and loss.

slides.notes: This is the most sensitive slide - handle it with sober honesty and respect for the victims, avoiding graphic detail. Present the famine and the purges factually as the human cost of Stalin's methods. Encourage FET learners to hold two facts together for analysis: real industrial and educational gains, alongside mass repression and death. Note that historians debate the numbers and the degree of intent behind the famine.

slides.title: Russia and the USSR, 1900-1940

blocks.alt: A timeline of key events in Russia from 1905 to the late 1930s.

events.label: 1905 Revolution: Bloody Sunday and the October Manifesto
February Revolution: Tsar Nicholas II abdicates
Stalin dominant; first Five-Year Plan begins

slides.notes: Use this slide to consolidate the whole unit's chronology before the review questions. Ask learners to identify the turning points and to group the events into the three phases: the collapse of Tsarism, the Bolshevik seizure and consolidation, and Stalin's transformation. Reinforce the Julian-to-Gregorian calendar point for February and October.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: In forty years Russia moved from tsarist autocracy to a communist superpower - transformed at great human cost.

blocks.text: The tsarist system collapsed under the weight of inequality, autocracy and the strain of World War I. The Bolsheviks seized and held power, and under Stalin the USSR was rapidly industrialised through collectivisation and the Five-Year Plans - achievements shadowed by famine, terror and dictatorship.

blocks.question: Explain why the Tsarist regime collapsed in 1917. Refer to long-term causes and the impact of World War I.
How did the Bolsheviks manage to seize and keep power between 1917 and 1921?
'Stalin transformed the USSR, but at too high a human cost.' Do you agree? Support your answer with evidence.

slides.notes: Close by returning to the unit's central question and the theme of transformation at a cost. The three questions rise in cognitive demand to match CAPS FET expectations: explanation of causation, analysis of the consolidation of power, and an evaluative essay-style judgement requiring a substantiated line of argument. Encourage learners to use specific evidence and to weigh both achievements and costs.
